questions.question: What are the three stages of the core Git workflow, in order?
Which command moves changes from the working directory to the staging area?
Which command saves staged changes permanently into the repository?
What does 'git status' do?
options: Shows what has changed in the working directory and staging area
Shows the difference between two commits
questions.answer: Shows what has changed in the working directory and staging area

questions.question: When you create a new file in a Git repository but have not run 'git add', what status does the file have?
In the output of 'git status', what color are untracked files typically shown in?
In the output of 'git status', what color are staged files typically shown in?
What is the staging area also known as?
Which command stages ALL changes in the current directory and its subdirectories?
What does 'git diff' (with no additional flags) show?
options: Staged changes that will be committed
Unstaged changes in the working directory
All changes since the last push
questions.answer: Unstaged changes in the working directory

questions.question: What does 'git diff --staged' show?
options: Unstaged changes in the working directory
Changes that have been staged and will be included in the next commit
Differences between the local and remote repository
Changes made since the repository was created
questions.answer: Changes that have been staged and will be included in the next commit

questions.question: If you modify a file, stage it with 'git add', and then run 'git diff', what will you see?
options: Nothing, because there are no unstaged changes
questions.answer: Nothing, because there are no unstaged changes

questions.question: Why is the staging area considered a powerful feature of Git?
options: It automatically backs up your files
It lets you choose exactly which changes to include in a commit
It compresses files to save disk space
questions.answer: It lets you choose exactly which changes to include in a commit

questions.question: In a diff output, what does a line starting with '+' indicate?
In a diff output, what does a line starting with '-' indicate?
You have modified file2.txt and file3.txt. You want to commit them as separate commits. What is the correct approach?
options: Run 'git add .' then 'git commit -m "Update both files"'
Run 'git add file2.txt', commit it, then 'git add file3.txt' and commit it
You cannot create separate commits for files changed at the same time
questions.answer: Run 'git add file2.txt', commit it, then 'git add file3.txt' and commit it

questions.question: What does 'git log' display?
options: A list of all files in the repository
The commit history with hashes, author names, and messages
A list of branches in the repository
The current status of the working directory
questions.answer: The commit history with hashes, author names, and messages

questions.question: What does 'git log --oneline' do differently from 'git log'?
options: It shows only the most recent commit
It shows one line per commit with just the hash and message
It shows commits from all branches
It shows commits in reverse chronological order
questions.answer: It shows one line per commit with just the hash and message

questions.question: Where does Git store its repository data?
After committing all changes and having no modifications, what does 'git status' report?
options: A list of all committed files

questions.question: You stage file1.txt and file2.txt but NOT file3.txt, then run 'git commit'. Which files are included in the commit?
options: No files, because all files must be staged

questions.question: What happens to an untracked file after you run 'git commit' without staging it first?
options: It is automatically included in the commit
It is deleted from the working directory
It remains untracked and is not included in the commit
Git displays an error and refuses to commit
questions.answer: It remains untracked and is not included in the commit

questions.question: How do you stage multiple specific files in a single command?
A file that Git already tracks has been edited. What status does 'git status' show for it?
Each commit in the Git log includes which unique identifier?
